summary: Typosquat of postcss-selector-parser ships AES-encrypted payload executed via new Function()
details: Package name postcss-minify-selector-parser mimics the widely-used postcss-selector-parser, and src/selector-parser.js transparently re-exports the real package (module.exports = require("postcss-selector-parser")) so the typosquat behaves as a drop-in. Beyond the legitimate re-export, the package ships a hidden execution surface: src/config/defaults.js contains DEFAULT_FINAL_ENCODED_TEXT, a base64 AES-256-GCM blob in iv:tag:ciphertext format, alongside hardcoded DEFAULT_AES_PASSPHRASE = "default-dev-passphrase" and salt "encode-npm-c-salt". src/pipeline/custom-codec-pipeline.js decrypts this blob and invokes new Function("require", runnable)(require), executing arbitrary code with full Node require() capability (network, filesystem, child_process). The README documents the package as a CSS selector parser and never mentions this run behavior. package.json wires this payload into scripts.start, scripts.test, and scripts.decode (all of them invoke node runtime/lib.min.js, which calls require("../src").run()), so the hidden code fires during npm test — the de-facto post-install verification step in most CI pipelines — reaching the installer's CI environment with full credentials and network access. Obfuscation + eval-equivalent + typosquat + undocumented exec API + auto-execution under npm test is the canonical trojaned-package shape.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
